coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of self-doped carbon dots with highly photoluminescence as multifunctional biosensors for detection of iron ions and pH
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Runxia
Wang, Xiufang
Sun, Yimin
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen doped carbon dots (C-dots) with highly photoluminescence (PL) are synthesized using m-aminobenzoic acid (MAC) as the only precursor by a simple, one-step hydrothermal method. The NH2 groups of MAC have been used as nitrogen source to dope C-dots. The synthesized nitrogen doped (N-doped) C-dots present an unexpectedly large quantum yield of 30.7% and possess an essentially crystalline nature and desirable functional groups. More important, the C-dots can serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect pH and Fe3+. The fluorescent intensity of the C-dots increases dramatically as pH increases from 2 to 10. The fluorescent paper with C-dots coated for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully prepared. Moreover, the C-dots are employed for assaying Fe3+ based on direct interactions between Fe3+ and COOH and NH2 groups of C-dots. Under the optimal condition, a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the concentration of Fe3+ is established in the range from 0 to 1.6μM. The detection limit is as low as 0.05μM, suggesting a promising assay for Fe3+. The results reported here provide a new approach for the design of N-doped multifunctional florescent sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Nitrogen doped
M-aminobenzoic acid
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-doped C-dots
Fluorescence response of N-doped C-dots toward pH
Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21301004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images, (c) size distribution, and (d) XRD pattern of synthesized N-doped C-dots.

(a) PL and UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared C-dots. Inset: photograph of C-dots aqueous solution taken under UV and visible light, and (b) PL spectra of C-dots excited by different wavelength.
FTIR spectrum (a), XPS scans of N-doped C-dots: (b) survey spectrum, (c) C 1s, and (d) N 1s.

(a) PL spectra of the N-doped C-dots dispersed in solutions with different pH values (from 2 to 11), (b) PL intensity at the peak position of the N-doped C-dots dispersed in solutions with different pH values, and (c) Photographs of filter paper with C-dots coating (below), and the pH values of the solutions dripped on the paper are 2, 4, 6 and 10 (up).
The reversible PL intensity of N-doped C-dots dispersion when the pH value alternates between 3 and 9.

(a) Evolution of fluorescence spectra of the C-dots in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.2, 1.6, 2.0, 2.4, and 2.8μM), and (b) plots of the fluorescence intensity against the concentration of Fe3+, I and I0 are the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively.
Selectivity investigation of the N-doped C-dots sensor for Fe3+ detection (ions concentration: 10μM).
Comparison of sensing performance of C-dots from different precursors for Fe3+detection.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of self-doped carbon dots with highly photoluminescence as multifunctional biosensors for detection of iron ions and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, Anhui, 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Building Materials, Anhui Jianzhu University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Building Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmacy department, Anhui Medical College, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The self-doped carbon dots with highly quantum yield are synthesized using m-aminobenzoic acid as the only precursor. The C-dots can serve as multifunctional fluorescence sensors to detect pH and the concentration of Fe3+.

The nitrogen doped carbon dots (C-dots) with highly photoluminescence (PL) are synthesized using m-aminobenzoic acid (MAC) as the only precursor by a simple, one-step hydrothermal method. The NH2 groups of MAC have been used as nitrogen source to dope C-dots. The synthesized nitrogen doped (N-doped) C-dots present an unexpectedly large quantum yield of 30.7% and possess an essentially crystalline nature and desirable functional groups. More important, the C-dots can serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect pH and Fe3+. The fluorescent intensity of the C-dots increases dramatically as pH increases from 2 to 10. The fluorescent paper with C-dots coated for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully prepared. Moreover, the C-dots are employed for assaying Fe3+ based on direct interactions between Fe3+ and COOH and NH2 groups of C-dots. Under the optimal condition, a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the concentration of Fe3+ is established in the range from 0 to 1.6μM. The detection limit is as low as 0.05μM, suggesting a promising assay for Fe3+. The results reported here provide a new approach for the design of N-doped multifunctional florescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photoluminescent C-dots have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability as well as low toxicity compared to the semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [1–3]. As new-generation photoluminescent nanomaterials, they have extensive potential applications in bioimaging, optoelectronics, photocatalysis and solar energy harvesting [4–7]. So, great efforts have been exerted on the preparation of C-dots from a variety of carbon precursors by various methods, such as arc-discharge, electrochemical oxidation, combustion, thermal decomposition or oxidation, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis [8–13]. However, most of these routes suffer from expensive equipments, harsh synthetic conditions, and tedious processes, leading to manufacturing difficulties and high costs for large scale production. Moreover, the resultant C-dots usually show low photoluminescence quantum yields, typically less than 10%, and the unfavorably low fluorescence quantum yield of C-dots inhibits their widespread use.

Basically, the photoluminescence behaviors of C-dots are related to their sizes, morphologies, compositions and crystalline degrees, which are decided by both the starting materials and the fabrication methods. Different pathways such as surface passivation, functionalization, and heteroatom doping have been proposed to adjust the surface features, the fluorescent behaviors, as well as the electrochemical performance of the resulting C-dots [14–17]. Among all these strategies, heteroatom-doping gained signiflcant research attention owing to its facile and easy manipulating nature. Especially, the doping of nitrogen atoms in C-dots provides the opportunities to adjust the structure and property of the C-dots. The various synthesis methods have been developed to fabricate N-doped C-dots, which generally can be categorized as two types: one is treating the as-made C-dots with N sources. But multiple steps are involved in such strategies, which will inevitably reduce the controllability over the compositions and the morphologies of the resulting N-doped C-dots. The other route is to directly utilize N containing molecules as the precursors of C-dots, which thus provides more options to adjust the structure and property of the N-doped C-dots. Yang et al. reported a facile method to prepare nitrogen-doped C-dots using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors under hydrothermal condition, which were applied in sensors and bioimaging [18]. Liu et al. successfully prepared C-dots with strongly yellow fluorescence through a microwave-assisted facile method using o-phenylenediamine as the carbon precursor. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescent behavior and excellent water-solubility due to some amino or hydroxy functional groups on the surface [19]. Yang et al. synthesized phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon dots by carbonization of adenosine-5′-triphosphate using a hydrothermal treatment, and the carbon dots were used as a novel sensing platform for live cell imaging of reactive oxygen species and reactive nitrogen species, including ClO−, ONOO−, and NO in macrophages [20]. Liu et al. fabricated highly photoluminescent nitrogen-rich carbon dots from melamine and citric acid for the selective detection of iron (III) ion [21]. Messina et al. used a multi-technique approach to study heavily nitrogen-doped C-dots synthesized by a simple bottom-up approach and capable of bright and color-tunable fluorescence in the visible region [22]. Huang et al. synthesized a series of C-dots with excitation-dependent emission and excitation-independent emission properties by controlling the content of nitrogen elements, confirming that the characteristic optical properties of C-dots originate from their energy levels [23]. Despite great efforts having been made, it is critical to search for new carbon sources to synthesize N-doped C-dots with highly photoluminescence using a simple strategy.

M-aminobenzoic acid with COOH and NH2 groups on the surface is an ideal precursor for the synthesis of C-dots. In the present work, MAC is employed as the sole precursor in the preparation of N-doped C-dots. The resulting C-dots are highly crystalline and the quantum yield of C-dots has been significantly improved to be 30.7%, much higher than the values of most reported doping-free and N-doped C-dots. The strategy is realized through a simple hydrothermal approach. It is remarkable that the C-dots can act as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensor to detect pH and Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution. Importantly, a portable paper based sensor has been prepared by simply coating the N-doped C-dots on the commercial filter paper, and visual fluorescence detection of pH by naked eyes is achieved under a UV lamp. The linear response range of Fe3+ detection is from 0 to 1.6μM, and the detection limit is 0.05μM. Moreover, the feasibility of C-dots for Fe3+ assay in local lake water is presented.

M-aminobenzoic acid is all analytical-grade reagents (Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China). The other reagents were used as received without further purification. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment to prepare the solutions.

The N-doped C-dots were synthesized from MAC through a simple, convenient and one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, MAC (0.2g) and water (20mL) were sealed into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, which was then placed in a drying oven (DHG-9123A, Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Limited Company) followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 12h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The purification of the C-dots was conducted through a dialysis tube (1000Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 24h. The final C-dots were redispersed in water with the concentration of 2mg/mL for the further application.

The QY of the as-synthesized N-doped C-dots was measured using quinine sulfate as the standard based on the following equation: QYC =QYR. (IC/AC) .(AR/IR) .(ηC/ηR)2
Q C
=
Q R
.
1
C
1
R
.
A R
A C
where QY stands for the quantum yield, I is the measured intensity of luminescent spectra, A is the optical density at excitation wavelength. Q was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.05M sulfuric acid solution (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 310nm η=1.33) as a standard for C-dots. The subscript “R” refers to standard with known quantum yield and “C” for the sample in this equation. The η is the refractive index of the solvent. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength.

A typical procedure for the detection of Fe3+ is described as follows: the fluorescence spectrum of 2mL C-dots suspension was recorded subsequently and the fluorescence intensity was set as F0. Then, different concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.2, 1.6, 2.0, 2.4, and 2.8μM) were mixed with the above solution under gentle shaking. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded and a series of I/I0 values were obtained. The other metal ions, such as Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Co2+, and Pb2+, etc., were also measured. All the detection experiments were carried out under the same conditions at room temperature. To evaluate the C-dots-based sensor for Fe3+ detection in a practical application, the performance of the present method for real water sample analysis was examined by lake water samples. The lake water samples were filtered through a 0.20μm filtered membrane and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to get rid of any insolubles. The Fe3+ at different concentration levels were added to lake water samples and analyzed with the proposed method.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of C-dots were obtained using TU-1800PC spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission spectra of C-dots were recorded in a microcell with 1cm path length using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi) with a quartzcell (1mm). The band pass for excitation and emission was set as 5nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a NEXUS-870 spectrophotometer with KBr pellet. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed by using a JEM-2100F instrument with a field emission gun operating at 200kV. The TEM specimens were prepared by placing one drop of the C-dots solution on carbon coated copper grid and were dried at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded with a Shimadu XRD-6000 powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056A°). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of C-dots were obtained with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). Photographs were taken with a canon 350D digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped C-dots were prepared by a simple, one-step hydrothermal method without further modification steps at 180°C for 12h. The purification of C-dots was through dialysis. The only reagent is m-aminobenzoic acid with amino and carboxyl groups, acting as the carbon source, which makes this method fascinating when compared with other N-doped C-dots preparations. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are recorded to analyze the morphology and structure of the N-doped C-dots. Fig. 1 a indicates that the C-dots appear relatively uniform in size. The average diameter of the C-dots is 3nm, and the size distribution accords well with Gaussian distribution by measuring 300 nanoparticles (Fig. 1c). The homogeneity of the C-dots, apparent in Fig. 1a, is notable since the synthesis strategy does not involve any purification or annealing steps, which is usually required in solution-based procedures in order to attain uniform size and shape distributions of N-doped C-dots. HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) shows clear lattice fringes, corresponding to spacing value of 0.28nm which is close to the value for the (020) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating high crystallinity of the N-doped C-dots. XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) exhibits a typical broad peak at about 24.9°, associated with the graphitic structure, which further confirms their crystalline nature. All these structural characterizations indicate that the C-dots have been successfully prepared.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the UV–vis and PL spectra of the resultant N-doped C-dots in aqueous solution. It is observed that two characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks are located at 300 and 407nm. The optical absorption peak at 300nm is typically assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core. The peak observed at 407nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition and the contribution of surface moieties. The as-prepared N-doped C-dots are faint yellow under daylight and emit a brighter blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365nm UV light (inset of Fig. 2a). The quantum yield of the C-dots is 30.7% using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as a reference, which is higher than that obtained using other additions as nitrogen source to dope C-dots [20,24]. In the prepared C-dots, the electron-rich nature of N atoms changes the charge density of C-dots, resulting in the energy transfer to the excited π* state of the sp2 cluster of C-dots. In the process of electron transfer from π* to π, higher energy is released, which causes a higher quantum yield. Upon excitation at the maximum excitation wavelength of 310nm, the PL spectrum exhibits a strong peak at 415nm.

The emission peaks of N-doped C-dots at various excitation wavelengths do not shift, indicating the uniformity of size and surface states (Fig. 2b). The constant emission wavelength also indicates that the as-prepared N-doped C-dots contain a single emitter only, which can avoid autofluorescence and is favorable for the application of C-dots in quantitative assays. The maximum emission wavelength at different excitation wavelengths remains at 415nm. The N-doped C-dots display the highest fluorescent intensity when they are excited at 310nm. Therefore, we select 310nm as the excitation wavelength for the following experiments.

The elemental composition of the N-doped C-dots is characterized by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum of the N-doped C-dots is shown in Fig. 3 a. Remarkably, the peak emerges at 3430cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH. The peak at 1709cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching vibrations, and the stretching vibration bands of CO at 1110 and 755cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups. The absorbance at 1630 and 3150cm−1 is assigned to NH bending vibration and NH stretching vibration, respectively, suggesting the existence of amino-containing functional groups. Moreover, two obvious absorption peaks at 1400 and 755cm−1 are assigned to CH bending vibrations. These peaks suggest that the N-doped C-dots have reasonable hydrophilic properties as well as considerable stability and dispersibility in water. This result reveals that the synthesized N-doped C-dots are co-functionalized by both amine and carboxylic groups.

XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3b) displays three dominant peaks at 285.0, 400.0 and 532.9eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, suggesting the existence of C, N and O elements. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c) can be resolved into four distinct carbon states at 284.6, 285.4, 286.2, and 288.4eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [25]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum reveals the signals of NH2 at 399.6eV and NC at 401.1eV (Fig. 3d) [26], indicating that C-dots are functionalized by NH2 groups. The XPS results are consistent well with the FTIR data and also indicate that the high degree of functionalization of the surface enables the N-doped C-dots particles to interact with the surface of many different kinds of materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-doped C-dots toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the resultant N-doped C-dots are highly sensitive to the pH values and possess a pH-sensitive PL feature. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, at lower pH, C-dots show low fluorescence intensity (pH<3). But the intensity increases rapidly as pH increases from 3 to 10, and when pH is above 10, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped C-dots does not change obviously. In the experiments, the amine moieties are protonated to ammonium salt (−NH3 +) at low pH, and the passivation effective of the amino group may be susceptible. On the other hand, the results may be attributed to the presence of the carboxyl groups. The carboxyl groups may be protonated in acidic solutions, which will lead to the aggregation of C-dots, resulting also in the fluorescence quenching [27]. This phenomenon further confirms that the amine and carboxyl groups are present on the surface of N-doped C-dots, which endows the C-dots with reasonable hydrophilic properties, stability and dispersibility in water.

We have made a C-dots coated paper for visual fluorescence detection of pH. A piece of filter paper is immersed into a C-dots solution for 1h. Then, the paper is removed from the solution and placed in an oven at 40°C for 1h. Under UV-lamp, this paper emits blue fluorescence (Fig. 4c, below), while the raw paper without any treatment is nonluminous, demonstrating the successful preparation of C-dots coated paper. The solutions with pH values of 2, 4, 6 and 10 are dripped on four pieces of fluorescent paper and air-dried for 20min (Fig. 4c, up). Under UV light, the paper dripped with the acidic solution (pH=2) is apparently white, and the paper with pH=3 presents a faint light blue. When pH is 6, the paper is apparently blue, and the region dripped with the alkaline solution (pH=10) exhibits an extra-bright blue fluorescence. The raw paper with alkaline solution (sodium hydroxide solution, pH=10) treatment after drying is nonluminous, which demonstrates the sodium hydroxide deposited on filter paper does not lead to distinct characteristic of fluorescence, and it does not interfere with our experimental result. Although the accurate pH assay using the C-dots coated paper has not been achieved, the distinct variation of fluorescence brightness on the paper can also indicate the rough pH values with naked eyes.

To evaluate the reversible behavior of the resultant C-dots upon switching between acid and base media, the pH-reversibility of the C-dots is shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the C-dots show a remarkable pH-switching PL property with no visible perturbation even after five cycles from pH 3 to pH 9, which indicates the high pH-reversibility. In short, we deem that the above observation and chemical structure analysis have connected the important variation of PL intensity to the protonation and deprotonation of the amino groups on the surface of the as-prepared N-doped C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Iron is of great importance for organisms and their biological systems and plays an important role in life. Thus, the detection of this transition metal ion is highly coveted due to the deepening crisis of industrial pollution. We have explored the feasibility of using the as-prepared N-doped C-dots for Fe3+ detection. Because the N-doped C-dots still have the strong PL intensity and robust chemical stability under acidic conditions reflected above, an acidic condition (pH=4.0) is adopted for detecting the metal ions. To study the sensitivity of C-dots for Fe3+ detection, different concentrations of Fe3+ are added to the aqueous solution of C-dots, and the fluorescence intensities are measured. As shown in Fig. 6 a, with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots decreases gradually. Fig. 6b shows the plot of fluorescence decreasing percentage vs the concentration of Fe3+. A nice linear response concentration range of Fe3+ is from 0 to 1.6μM, while the quantification of Fe3+ can be achieved with standard deviation R2 =0.9976, and the lowest detection limit (LOD) is about 0.05μM based on the standard deviation of the response (σ) and the slope of the calibration curve (S) at levels approximating the LOD according to the formula: LOD=3.3(σ/S), which is lower than the previously reported results [21,26,28–30]. The comparison of LOD of Fe3+ using C-dots from different carbon source has been listed in Table 1 . Notably, the ultrasensitive detection of Fe3+ using the N-doped C-dots can be achieved in the aqueous solution.

The C-dots based fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ assay in river water samples is also explored in this work. The standard Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations are added to the pretreated water sample, and then analyze with the proposed method. Fig. S1 of Supplementary information in the online version at DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.10.043 shows the fluorescence response of C-dots in presence of river water containing different concentrations of Fe3+ ions and the corresponding relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Fe3+. Similar to the above results, the PL intensities of C-dots show a trend of decreasing with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+. A good linear correlation has also been obtained in the range of 0–1.2μM for the river water (R2 =0.9890). The results demonstrate that the proposed sensor is capable of detecting Fe3+ in real environmental samples.

The C-dots based fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ assay in river water samples is also explored in this work. The standard Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations are added to the pretreated water sample, and then analyze with the proposed method. Fig. S1 shows the fluorescence response of C-dots in presence of river water containing different concentrations of Fe3+ ions and the corresponding relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Fe3+. Similar to the above results, the PL intensities of C-dots show a trend of decreasing with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+. A good linear correlation has also been obtained in the range of 0–1.2μM for the river water (R2 =0.9890). The results demonstrate that the proposed sensor is capable of detecting Fe3+ in real environmental samples.
Fig. S1

To evaluate the selectivity of this sensing system, we have examined the PL intensity change in the presence of representative metal ions under the same conditions including Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Co2+, and Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 7 , only Fe3+ ions can significantly quench the ratiometric luminescence output of the C-dots. These results suggest that the N-doped C-dots sensor exhibits a high selectivity to Fe3+. Fe3+ is considered to be coordinated with amino, carboxyl groups on the surface of C-dots, and the radiative transition is disrupted and thus leads to the PL quenching [21]. Moreover, the fluorescence decay dynamics are evaluated to prove the fluorescence quenching mechanism. According to fluorescence lifetime curves, the average lifetimes of C-dots and C-dots/Fe3+ are 8.02ns and 7.90ns, which present a slight change, while their PL spectra have changed obviously (Fig. 6a). This indicates that the quenching mechanism of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ is static quenching.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized highly fluorescent N-doped C-dots by hydrothermal method with m-aminobenzoic acid as the precursor and nitrogen source. The synthesized N-doped C-dots present large quantum yield, crystalline nature and desirable functional groups. The C-dots can serve as fluorescent nanosensors to detect pH. A fluorescent paper with C-dots coated for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully prepared. Moreover, the N-doped C-dots are employed for assaying Fe3+, and a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the concentration of Fe3+ is established in the range from 0 to 1.6μM. The detection limit is as low as 0.05μM, suggesting a promising assay for Fe3+. The C-dots applied for Fe3+ assay in real water samples is achieved. This work provides a new route for low-cost synthesis of ultrabright N-doped C-dots from cheap precursors. We believe that the proposed N-doped C-dots sensors will be more useful upon further development.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21301004).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Runxia Wang is a professor at Anhui Medical College, China. Her current research interests focus on biosensor.
Xiufang Wang is an associate professor at School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University, China. Her current research interests focus on biosensor and photocatalyst.
Yimin Sun is a professor at Anhui Normal University,China. His current research interests focus on material science.
